data.title: Data Centres And Debt: How The AI Building Boom Is Reshaping Credit Markets
data.description:
As AI companies raise billions of dollars to finance AI infrastructure projects, corporate bond supply is surging. Rachana Bhat, Vice President and Director, Investment Grade Credit Portfolio Management, TD Asset Management, joins MoneyTalk to discuss the long-term growth and potential refinancing risks of AI’s building boom.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: The AI hyperscalers have unveiled billions of spending this year, with some companies doubling the amount they plan to spend on development compared to last year. That news hasn't been taken all that well by the equity market this round. How is the bond market viewing all this? Joining us now to discuss is Rachana Bhat, VP and director of Investment-Grade Credit Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management.

Rachana, always great to have you here with us.

Rachana Bhat: Likewise. Great to be here. And Happy Friday.

Greg Bonnell: Happy Friday, indeed. AI keeps being the big story. The hyperscalers had been promising, heading to this year, big spending. We just came out of an earnings period. What did they actually tell us about their spending plans?

Rachana Bhat: Yeah, Greg, so what we've learned is, the spending is not slowing-- it's accelerating. So when you look at the big hyperscalers, which is Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), i.e., Google-- there's Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), and Oracle (ORCL). They are spending-- or they came into the year expecting that they'll spend more than $500 billion combined. But that number was revised to more than $650 billion. So that's up about 65% since last year's spending.

Now, most of that upside surprise in capex has actually come from Amazon and Google-- so thankfully, companies that have very, very solid balance sheets. Now, that's for the year. Now, when you think about, call it, the next five years, the spending expectation is for $5 trillion. So let's say, on an average, $1 trillion combined for those five hyperscalers.

Let me contextualize this for you. If you take S&P 500 in 2024, added all the capex for the companies back then, the number would have been $950 billion-- so less than what these five companies are spending just by themselves on an average over the next five years.

So yes, this is very large, concentrated investment cycle. And that's what makes it very important as well. These companies are spending to build out the technical infrastructure to support AI and cloud demand. So a lot of these companies, you'll see, will shift to negative net free cash flow-- which is free cash flow minus capex minus shareholder returns. What happens when your free cash flow is negative? Well, you need to borrow more money. So they'll be issuing a lot more debt as they fund this buildout over the coming years.

Greg Bonnell: Now, last year, maybe some exceptions, but every time one of the big hyperscalers came and said, man, we're going to spend so much money, the market said, we love it. They rewarded the share price. This latest round, though, these big numbers that came out-- and we were warned they'd be big numbers-- the stocks would pull back on that news. So that's the equity side. What about the bond side? Are bond sides concerned about the fundamentals here, the bond investors?

Rachana Bhat: Yeah, so what was interesting for me, even on the equity side is, if you thought about this a couple of months ago, numbers like this alongside the earnings beats that we've seen was driving a lot of enthusiasm. Now, the same numbers are being seen from a negative lens now. And that's because the investors are now focused a lot more on return on investment, the path to monetization. And rightfully so-- because this capex is accelerating, and it's rising from very elevated levels.

Now, when you shift to the credit side, or the bond side, if you look at-- things have been calm as compared to equities. When you look at the tech-- so IG tech sector spreads, they're actually flat on the year, very much in line with what the broader market has done. So the IG tech sector spreads over this year have actually been flat.

The big reason for that is these companies-- with the exception of Oracle-- are sitting on tons of cash, a lot of free cash flow. Yes, I said that free cash flow will be going negative as we move on over the next few years, but the idea is that these companies have a lot of room on their balance sheets before that downgrade happens. And even if that downgrade happens, well, guess what? They will still be in a very high-quality bucket.

I'll give you an example. So, S&P recently affirmed Google's ratings at AA plus. They said, well, guess what-- Google can come and issue 180 billion in net incremental debt before there's a downgrade concern. For context, Google this year is expected to issue about $30-35 billion, which, for the most part, it's already done. So technically, it means Google can come five more times, given that $180 billion number--

Greg Bonnell: Not get anyone nervous.

Rachana Bhat: Exactly, before that downgrade happens. And guess what? Even if that downgrade happens, you're just going from AA plus to AA mid. For these companies to be downgraded to BBB-- again, with the exception of Oracle-- Google will have to drive truckloads of debt before that even happens.

So, very different story than what you have seen within equities. And because of that strong fundamental, the balance sheet, the ratings buffer these guys have, the strong balance sheets they have, IG credit as an asset class can actually withstand a lot more in terms of, call it, the AI disruption or monetization challenges and be more resilient-- which is why you've seen tech IG sector spreads hold up pretty well.

Greg Bonnell: So those are big numbers in terms of issuance, just for Google alone. Of course, there's several of them that are going to be issuing debt. And some of it's a hundred-year debt. Hundred-year bonds. Is there demand out there to meet this issuance?

Rachana Bhat: You've done your homework. So, oh my God, tons of demand. Tons of demand. So, if anything, the problem is quite the opposite, which is they're coming out with so much supply of bonds, but we as investors are not getting enough.

Take Google. So Google came to market and said, hey, I want to raise $15 billion in debt in the US investment-grade market. The demand? $100 billion. So they upsized to $20 billion. They took $20 billion in the proceeds from this debt-raising cycle. Well, it was easy for them, so it makes sense. Great job, right, from Google's perspective.

Now, you pointed to the hundred-year bond. So what's really interesting for Google is they actually also went to the sterling market to issue about-- call it more than 5 billion in debt. And one of the tranches there for what they issued was a hundred-year bond. Very, very, very rare for corporates. It's usually mostly reserved for governments, high-quality governments and, call it, long-established universities. For example, Oxford University has a hundred-year bond.

Greg Bonnell: What's going to be around in a hundred years is the question, right?

Rachana Bhat: So Oxford University has been there since 1300s, and it's structurally important for the country. So it makes sense. But Google, that's a tech company issuing a hundred-year bond. So to me, from my perspective, this is a very bullish signal, if you will, from Google's perspective, because they're issuing at very high interest rates. So they're showing you confidence in their AI journey that they will be able to get this money back. And the demand for that hundred-year bond, by the way, that tranche was greater than nine times oversubscribed. So, pretty impressive there.

You know, people can still have concerns around, oh, can these companies still come to the market, and are there funding concerns? We've heard a lot around that. I would tell them to look at Oracle. So Oracle, as an example, is everything what market was concerned about-- high leverage, but leverage still rising up. And then you have OpenAI concentration, uncertainty around OpenAI being able to pay them in the future, and lots of debt that was at risk of falling into high yield from investment-grade. So, many bad things. Look what happens.

So Oracle comes to market wanting to issue $25 billion in debt. The demand? $130 billion. So really, no demand concerns out there, especially given, if you think about these companies-- again, outside of Oracle-- they have choice whether they want to come and issue in this market because they have lots of cash and free cash flow. So they can say, well, the market is not behaving well. I'm going to step back. I don't need to issue. So that optionality really helps.

And to me, there are three things that are really supporting that demand. So the first one is-- if you could bring up the chart now-- the first one is, if you look at the tech investment-grade sector spreads, look at that arrow. It's already jumped up. And for clarity, spreads are the extra compensation you earn when you buy a corporate bond over a government bond. So that compensation has risen, as you can see. So it's already adjusted for this supply expectation, if you will.

The second reason is we as investors-- this has been very well-publicized and written about-- so we as investors were positioned for this supply.

The third is balance sheet. As we discussed, the fundamentals are in pretty good shape. And where they are not-- which is in the case of Oracle-- we as investors are getting well-compensated for it. So yes, the demand story is quite supportive from an investment-grade lens.

Greg Bonnell: Now, if you go back to the pullback we saw on some of the equity side of the hyperscalers on these plans, I mean, there's 2-3% on any given day. The sharper selloff has been in the software stocks because of all the fear that AI is going to come in and just disrupt their businesses to the point where people will use the AI and not them. So a lot of risk, fear there on the equity side. Is any of that showing up in the broader credit market?

Rachana Bhat: Yeah, so what's been fascinating for me, Greg, is that when you look at the equity market, it's not just the disruptors, so guys who are actually building or helping build the AI infrastructure, but also the disruptees, which are the software services companies which will be at risk if this buildup is successful. The stocks were getting hit at the same time. So that was very fascinating for me.

Now, when you look at this from a credit lens, there are real concerns. But those concerns, from a credit standpoint, are not evenly distributed. What I mean by that is that pressure is really concentrated within low-quality, smaller companies that have very high leverage. Why? Because high leverage, they need to pay debt. You have these companies that are being challenged. Their business models are being challenged. Their stock valuations are falling. If stock valuations are falling, that means their underlying asset values are falling. And in that case, your credit metrics are weakening a lot more, and very quickly. So this could actually take these companies towards default as well. So, it's very important to be careful of those pockets.

Now, those pockets are actually concentrated within leveraged loan and private credit. But they are lower-quality pockets in those, call it, asset classes-- about 15% to 20% exposure within leveraged loans and private credit to these smaller companies that are highly leveraged. In high yield, that exposure is less than 5%.

What's important to watch here, in my view, is the refinancing activity. So there's refinancing wall coming in 2028, which is a big wall. How and when they tackle those will be important to watch, especially with all of what's been going on.

But one of the things that I think about right now is, the lower you go in quality, the more dispersion there is within fundamentals and valuation. So, it's extremely, extremely important to be selective. For example, if you look at high yield on this, it has less than 5% exposure. So it is pretty insulated relative to, call it, leveraged loans and private credit.

High yield, on the surface, has not really widened much for the year. But when you look at high-yield tech sector, which is where some of these smaller software companies are concentrated, high-yield sector spreads or high-yield tech sector spreads have widened by a hundred basis points.

So selectivity really matters. You can't just close your eyes and buy the index. It is a stock picker's market. It is a bond picker's market. And having, call it, experienced managers manage it for you, that have the resources, big teams that are looking through financials, cash flows, covenants, coverage is extremely important.

Now, when you look at investment-grade-- let's shift to investment-grade a little bit here-- the problem credits are far and few because we don't have small companies within investment-grade. Most of the exposure within investment-grade comes indirectly through BDC exposure. But that BDC exposure is less than 1% of the IG market. So, quite insulated on that front.

So when I think about the broader credit space, to me, investment-grade really provides that resilience and has a strong role to play in portfolios today, because I can earn that steady, relatively steady income without all the worry that's going on in the market because of all the solid fundamentals that we discussed.

data.title: Realty Income: $14 Trillion Market, Monthly Dividends
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

When was the last time you took a quick trip to the store?

Perhaps you were driving to your final destination and realized you needed to grab something. So you parked your car, ran into a CVS (CVS) or Walgreens, ran back out to your car, and continued on your way. Perhaps you're missing an item from your pantry that you need for your meal, so you visit a grocery store, whether that be Albertsons (ACI), Walmart (WMT) Neighborhood Market, or a Publix.

What do all of these experiences have in common? With each one of them, you went to a physical location to complete your purchase.

That physical location had to be owned by someone, whether that was the store itself owning its property or whether that store, more likely, was leasing that property from someone else who owned it.

Millions of Americans every single day step foot inside a physical retail location without a second thought of who potentially owns that location. They're simply conducting their day-to-day business, and stores have to have a physical presence if they want to capture that specific stream of business. If I need something right now, I can't go on Amazon and buy it. Because I need it today, which means I need to go get the tangible item from a physical location. The net lease sector is fragmented. There are many players and plenty of opportunities, and often the biggest players can unlock the best opportunities because of their size and scale.

Today, I want to look at a Dividend Aristocrat who has the ability to capitalize on wonderful opportunities within this sector. Who knows, perhaps the last time you ran into a store, you were running along a property they own.

Let's dive in!

A Dividend Aristocrat With A $14 Trillion Runway

Realty Income (O) owns over 15,500 commercial real estate properties throughout the U.S., the U.K., and Continental Europe. O offers the benefits of real estate ownership without the inconveniences that our friend Brad Thomas often mentions: toilets, trash, and taxes.

The triple net lease REIT has professionals who oversee everything for shareholders. Portfolio management. Sourcing prospective acquisition volume. Vetting it. Executing the deals. You name it.

All the while, the business is structured so that tenants pay the taxes, utilities, maintenance, and insurance and send a rent check to O each month. The icing on the cake is that these leases are often lengthy (with a weighted average lease term of 8.9 years) and come with annual lease escalators.

Unsurprisingly, this business model is very lucrative for O. As we discussed with our members in a December article, the net lease REIT's AFFO margin was 67.5% in Q3 2025. In other words, two out of every three dollars of rent checks drop straight to the bottom line.

Tenants agree to these terms because sale-leasebacks allow them to unlock the value of their real estate. This lighter asset base frees them up for business expansion, debt repayment, etc.

O's business is versatile, too. The net lease REIT owns everything from 7/11 convenience stores to a stake in the iconic Bellagio hotel and casino in Las Vegas.

Looking ahead, the company is operating in a $14 trillion combined net lease total addressable market between the U.S. and Europe. This gives O plenty of room for further acquisitions.

That's why its accelerating acquisition volumes ($1.4 billion at a 7.7% weighted average initial cash yield in Q3 2025) and annual lease escalators give us confidence in its growth outlook. The FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for AFFO per share to grow by over 3% annually through 2028, off an anticipated 2025 base of $4.26.

O's A- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook provides it with a cost of capital advantage as well. This makes it easier for the REIT to find accretive deals without taking on excessive risk.

O's 5.2% dividend yield is also enticing. The AFFO payout ratio for 2026 is likely to be in the mid-70% range. That should allow O to maintain its five-year dividend CAGR of 3.5%, which works out to a Chowder number approaching 9. We put O into the same category as utilities for our Chowder Number evaluation. This is due to its utility-esque earning stability and growth pattern. This would build on a 30-plus-year dividend growth streak as a publicly traded company.

O looks to be trading at a solid valuation as well. From the current $66 share price (as of Feb. 17, 2026), the stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio of 15.33. For more perspective, this is materially less than the FAST Graphs 10-year average P/AFFO ratio of 18.3.

Of course, we don't believe that a return to the 10-year average valuation multiple is likely. Not because of a deterioration in the fundamentals (the 4.1% annual long-term FactSet AFFO per share growth consensus is comparable to the 10-year CAGR of 5.3%).

Rather, the environment of a 4%+ 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) acts as a drag on the valuation multiple. That's why we think that a fair value P/AFFO ratio of 16.5 (one standard deviation below the 10-year average) is realistic. This would imply shares are worth $73 apiece and are currently 14% discounted.

If O returns to fair value and matches growth forecasts, it could post a 15% total return in what's left of 2026. Through 2030, O has a realistic path to 9% annual total returns.

Of course, the REIT doesn't come without its risks. As we discussed a few months ago, key risks to O include the potential for an inflation ramp-up and the geographic concentration of its portfolio.

Currently at 1%, the Truflation U.S. CPI Index is at the lowest point that we can remember coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic. If inflation did pick back up, though, the 10-year yield could move higher, causing at least a temporary compression in investment spreads. That could lead to a temporary slowdown in acquisition volumes, which would weigh on O's growth potential.

The other risk is that 37.6% of O's ABR was derived from its top five markets (page 57 of 66 of O's Q3 2025 10-Q Filing): The U.K. (13.9%), Texas (9.6%), Florida (5.2%), California (4.7%), and Illinois (4.2%).

Any unfavorable economic or demographic occurrences in these markets could hurt its tenants. In the process, some of these tenants may experience financial difficulties. This could impact O's financial results.

Conclusion

It's likely very few Americans ever realize that they're running across a property owned by O because they're more focused on the name on the door than on who owns the property below their feet. That works to the benefit of shareholders. Often, I find it is better to take a step back from the day-to-day consumer-facing activities and enjoy the benefits of owning real estate, owning infrastructure, and providing services to those who have to deal with consumers instead of the consumers themselves.

When building a portfolio that will house my financial security throughout my life in retirement, I want that portfolio to be of the utmost quality and reliability. I want it to be a fortress on a hill, protected from all sides but giving me clear insight and vision as to what may come. You can have a portfolio exactly like that as well.

Become a Dividend King. Live like dividend royalty.

data.title: Alibaba: Risk Outweighs Reward In Its Q3 Earnings Despite AI Chips
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The fact is Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) (BABAF) has generated only +65% in stock price appreciation since its IPO twelve years ago, and I believe the company will likely continue to underperform in light of its modest growth and compressed margins, despite huge AI tailwinds. The +30% rally in the last twelve months only worsened the buy case with a starker valuation in light of earnings that didn’t follow. The fact that it is trading today at a 20% discount from its 52-week high of almost $200 per share is, in my opinion, not an opportunity but a reflection of the disappointment.

I will dive into the recent earnings, as well as product updates and the outlook of Alibaba's earnings. Spoiler alert: as a former shareholder who enjoyed the e-commerce boom during the lockdown, I think it’s still not time to jump back into this AI laggard. For the reasons I will detail further in the article, the stock will remain, in my opinion, an underperformer, and I am therefore initiating a Sell rating ahead of its Q3 2026 earnings release.

Business Overview and Growth Breakdown

Alibaba’s business model is a conglomerate of three core segments: its Alibaba China E-commerce Group (primarily Taobao and Tmall), its Cloud Intelligence Group (Alibaba Cloud, proprietary T-Head AI Chips), and its Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group (“AIDC,” primarily AliExpress and a few other similar international platforms).

On the surface, the revenue figures show a healthy growth with 15% of total revenue growth on a YoY basis based on the latest second-quarter results of 2026. The core business, which is e-commerce (both the China market and AIDC), showed somewhat moderate growth at 16% YoY revenue growth for China and 10% growth for AIDC. The tech business segment (Cloud Intelligence) had much stronger growth at +34% YoY thanks to major tailwinds and some positive developments. As I mentioned, on the surface, the picture looks good.

But as Peter Lynch’s famous quote goes:

“If you can follow only one bit of data, follow the earnings—assuming the company in question has earnings. I subscribe to the crusty notion that sooner or later, earnings make or break an investment in equities. What the stock price does today, tomorrow, or next week is only a distraction.”

And exactly there lies my concern as a former shareholder of BABA shares. When looking at the business as a whole, including the disposed Sun Art and Intime businesses, the revenue growth was much more moderate at 5% YoY. While selling underperforming businesses to focus on the faster-growing ones makes absolute sense for shareholder value creation, what worries me is when digging a little deeper in the financials.

Income from operations dropped 85% YoY in Q2, showing a worrying acceleration of the decline relative to the six months ending September 30. Earnings are truly in free fall mode despite having potentially the greatest tailwinds in terms of AI and technology of recent years. The comment of management is equally worrisome, saying the free fall in earnings is “primarily attributable to the investment in quick commerce, user experiences, and technology.” This means the deterioration of the business is very broad and induced by a variety of factors. The most likely ones are Trump tariffs on China affecting its international e-commerce business, the fierce competition in China threatening its national market shares amidst weak Chinese consumers, and the over-reliance of Alibaba’s technology group on imports from abroad, in particular Nvidia’s H200 GPU chips.

Q3 2026 Earnings Outlook and What Could Invalidate My Sell Rating

The third-quarter earnings of 2026, which will be released on Friday, February 20 (pre-market), are supposed to demonstrate somewhat better earnings per share after the businesses of Sun Art and Intime have been disposed of. These are most likely going to be inorganic (related to said dispositions) rather than any proper earnings catalysts. Indeed, despite improved earnings expected for Q3 relative to Q2 ($1.58 per share expected relative to $0.62 in Q2, which missed by $0.20), the EPS revisions have been pointing downwards.

With 26 down revisions against only 3 up revisions, the bearish sentiment is taking over, backed by weak earnings and margins. While the most recent net income margin is at 12.19%, when considering the levered FCF, we are in negative territory at -4.89%, which, as I mentioned previously, is concerning given the number of tailwinds in the tech sector.

One key factor that could invalidate my Sell rating, which could make me want to be a shareholder of BABA stock again, is if the company announces that (for example) its AI chips have generated a few percentage points of earnings and are opening a path to earnings. Their T-Head chips, a hoped-for alternative to Nvidia’s chips, will be key in the earnings release. A report has recently floated the idea that its AI Chips business may be preparing for an IPO, which could help raise billions of dollars for Alibaba. On the other hand, selling the key growth catalyst, while beneficial in the short term because of the liquidity, leaves little reason for the long-term shareholders to hold BABA stock.

Balance Sheet and Valuation

From a balance sheet perspective, the company is healthy, with total assets of $264.5 billion far exceeding total liabilities of $108.5 billion. The liquidity ratio is also healthy at 1.5x. However, the trajectory may show early signs of a company that is using more and more of its cash, while both growth rates and profitability rates, as we saw above, are not showing clear results of such spending rates.

Indeed, total cash and short-term investments have been in free fall in the AI era, from $70+ billion in 2021-2024 to $59 billion in March 2025 and $52 billion in September 2025. The company has been spending an enormous amount of cash to sustain its businesses and develop its AI chips, which makes this week’s earnings release even more crucial in terms of ROC (currently at a mere 5% per the last report).

Lastly, from a valuation perspective, the stock is trading well above its historical median, meaning the recent rally in stock price has been fuelled by a valuation multiples expansion only, rather than by earnings. So much so that the P/E stands at 17x when considering 2025 earnings and 17x when considering estimates of 2027 earnings. P/E based on 2026 earnings would even inflate to 24+. To put this into perspective, BABA’s 5-year median stands at 13x earnings. In my view, too much is at stake in the earnings release on Friday, with risk exceeding reward, and any long position taken prior to the release would be speculative. There is another big player that I prefer: JD.com, Inc. (JD). JD is also developing a promising AI chip alternative to Nvidia, has a P/E of around 9-10x, and has a revenue growth of 16.6%.

Bottom Line

Bottom line, BABA remains a Sell until proof of the contrary. The AI chips were supposed to be a major earnings catalyst, but as for the last Q2 earnings report, the figures disappointed the markets. Revisions have been pointing downwards, and unless there is a clear earnings turnaround announced this Friday relative to Q3, the stock remains a Sell. Additionally, if earnings do bounce up, the future of its AI chips business remains an uncertainty with a big question mark. An IPO could generate liquidity (which has been in a free fall in the last quarters), but which growth catalysts would then remain for its long-term shareholders?

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Somnigroup International's Pullback Doesn't Represent An Opportunity (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
One very interesting prospect over the last few years is a company that currently goes by the name Somnigroup International (SGI). In the past, the firm went by the name Tempur Sealy International. But in 2025, management completed the merger of that business with Mattress Firm, consolidating the two businesses into a true powerhouse in the betting space. Over the last few years, I had been bullish about this enterprise. In fact, since I last called its predecessor, Tempur Sealy International, a ‘buy’ back in May of 2023, this stock has jumped 146.7%. That easily outpaces the 65.4% rise that the market experienced. And since I originally turned bullish on it in March of 2021, the stock jumped 102.8%. That's more than double the 45.9% rise that the market enjoyed.

These moves higher are after taking into consideration the 8.6% plunge in pricing that the stock saw on February 17th. On that day, shares dropped because management reported financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Unfortunately, revenue and earnings per share both fell short of analysts’ expectations. And in all likelihood, even though the business should achieve solid growth this year, that's not enough given the valuation of the company to justify strong upside. In fact, based on how the stock is priced, not only on an absolute basis but relative to have companies that have some similarities to it, I would say that the absolute most generous assessment that I can give it is a ‘hold’ rating at this time.

A tough quarter

At first glance, the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year looks like it was a great time for shareholders. Take revenue as an example. It came in at $1.87 billion. That is drastically above the $1.21 billion that the company reported a year earlier. However, it fell short of analysts’ expectations to the tune of $62.4 million. Still, you might argue, this rise in sales was drastic. However, the devil is in the details. Revenue only increased this much because of the company's purchase of Mattress Firm. For context, that competitor in the bedding space was acquired in February of last year in a deal valued at $5.1 billion. $2.8 billion of that was cash, while the rest was in the form of 34.2 million shares. This alone accounted for $892.1 million for the quarter. Without it, revenue would have actually fallen 19.2% year over year.

The big pain point here ended up being the company’s Tempur Sealy North America segment. Overall revenue plunged from $894.1 million to $620.5 million. The good news from this drop was that it was largely driven by the company's decision to sell off Sleep Outfitters. That alone accounted for a $273.6 million decline in revenue for the business.

If not for that, actual revenue for the company, on an organic basis, would have risen by about $42 million. The organic rise in sales for the company, then, came from the Tempur Sealy International segment, with revenue growing from $313.8 million to $355.8 million. Most of this was attributable to the direct channel for the company, though it did see some expansion on the wholesale side. Management attributed this overall growth to expanded distribution activities.

The bottom line for the company experienced significant expansion as well. Earnings per share shot up from $0.40 to $0.66, taking net profits for the business up from $71.9 million to $140.8 million. Sadly, even with this rise, earnings per share fell short of what analysts were expecting to the tune of $0.06. But there is some good news here. Adjusted earnings per share popped from $0.60 to $0.72, falling in line with what analysts were hoping to see. This meant a rise in adjusted net profits for the company from $107.2 million to $153.7 million. Other profitability metrics for the business largely increased. The one exception to this was operating cash flow. It contracted from $129.1 million to $99.4 million. But if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $112.5 million to $243.4 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $219.4 million to $348.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the last three completed fiscal years. Because of the purchase of Mattress Firm, I would argue that data prior to 2025 is obsolete. But it does go to illustrate that the business has a history of expansion. What's more, management seems optimistic about the near term outlook for the company. For context, the North American betting market is worth about $45 billion. In international markets, it's even larger at $75 billion. Here in North America, the company is a market share leader. That helps to explain the vast majority of its revenue comes from the US. In fact, its retail footprint stands at around 2,100 stores spread across 45 states. And also has an integrated e-commerce business that receives in excess of 75 million web visitors each year. However, the company does sell its products to more than 100 countries across the globe. And width a low single digit piece of the overall market internationally, there's plenty of growth there. That helps to explain why management continues to make investments internationally. One example would be its Dreams platform, where the company operates 225 brick and mortar retail stores in the UK.

This was a retail establishment that Tempur Sealy International acquired back in 2021. And today, the 225 brick and mortar locations that it has, as well as its e-commerce presence, makes it a meaningful piece of the overall business. In fact, its e-commerce platform draws in over 18 million visitors every year. Management views this as a growth opportunity in the years to come. The goal that the firm laid out is for management to leverage its vertically integrated business model and its wide array of offerings in order to attract consumers in the markets in which those assets are focused. Overall investments in growth opportunities made by the company have exceeded $800 million in the last several years. This specifically refers to investments made in plants and processes in order to boost sales and improve profit margins. This is in addition to the $5.7 billion dedicated to mergers and acquisitions activities.

Continued integration of Mattress Firm is also a major priority of the company. You see, in addition to targeting sales growth from that acquisition, the company also was hoping to achieve significant cost savings. In 2025, it achieved about $20 million of cost synergies, in addition to sales synergies that boosted EBITDA by $60 million. For 2026, the company is targeting $55 million of additional cost savings and $40 million of EBITDA synergies coming from sales. And next year, the forecast is for another $50 million in cost savings. If everything goes according to plan, over this three-year window of time, the company hopes to capture $225 million in EBITDA improvements.

If the company can achieve these lofty targets, the end goal would be adjusted earnings per share of $5.15 by 2028. That would mark a big improvement over the $3.20 per share estimated at the midpoint for this year. The math here is muddied by the fact that management has a history of buying back stock. So we don't actually know what overall adjusted profits will be at that time if the company achieves its targets. But if we keep the number of shares outstanding unchanged, this would mean a jump in adjusted net income from $565.3 million in 2025 to $1.10 billion by 2028. This would translate to adjusted operating cash flow of around $1.45 billion and to EBITDA of about $2.55 billion. For this year, we are looking at a target of about $684.8 million. The company should also achieve adjusted operating cash flow of about $996.7 million and EBITDA of $1.45 billion.

Using all of these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares of the business are currently priced. Unfortunately, business looks quite lofty on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms. In fact, in the chart below, you can see how it stacks up against five companies that are probably the closest to it that we can get from a comparable basis. On a price to earnings basis, four of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is. And all of them were cheaper when it came to the other two valuation approaches.

Takeaway

Based on all of the data provided right now, it is my assertion that while Somnigroup International it's an interesting business, it's not one that I can get terribly bullish about right now. Operationally speaking, the company is doing a fine job. And if management can achieve the targets that they have set out for themselves for the next couple of years, meaningful upside could exist for investors. But sadly, because of how pricey the stock is, I even considered taking a bearish stance. It was really only because of the promise of additional profitability improvements over the next few years, and the valuation that would imply, that I refrained from doing so. Instead, I think that rating it a ‘hold’ is the appropriate course of action.

data.title: TTM Technologies: After Big Run, Still Has Room To Grow
data.description:
TTM Technologies (NASDAQ: TTMI) has been transforming from a traditional printed circuit board (PCB) manufacturer into a critical supplier for AI‑driven hardware infrastructure, defense systems, and high‑performance electronics. Over the past year, the stock has generated extraordinary returns — more than 260% year‑over‑year — and has captured the attention of Wall Street analysts who now rate it a Strong Buy while still having significant upside projected for the future.

Seeking Alpha analysts have a "Buy" rating on the company, while Seeking Alpha quant has a 'Strong Buy' rating.

In this article we'll look at the company’s fundamentals, future prospects, strategic positioning, and valuation.

What TTMI is and does

TTM Technologies is a U.S.-based manufacturer specializing in high‑performance electronics for data centers and industrial applications, microelectronic assemblies, mission‑critical systems for aerospace and defense, printed circuit boards (PCBs), and RF and microwave components

The company operates through two primary segments: printed circuit boards, which drives most of the company's revenue. It includes a wide variety of products, layout design, simulations, and testing services.

The second major segment is RF & Specialty Components, which includes RF components, microelectronics and microwave assemblies. While generating less revenue, it is a fast-growing segment of the company.

As for geographically, approximately 50% of its revenue comes from the U.S. market, with the other half primarily coming from global markets in aerospace, defense, industrial, and networking.

Latest earnings

In its latest earnings report for Q4, 2025, the company produced revenue of $774.3 million, up 18.8% year-over-year, beating expectations by $21.34 million. In Q4 of 2024, the company generated revenue of $651.00 million.

GAAP net income was $50.7 million, or $048 per diluted share. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.70, beating estimates by $0.02. Last year in the same reporting period the company had net income of $5.2 million, or $0.05 per diluted share.

Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 2025 was $126.2 million, accounting for 16.3% of net sales, in comparison to EBITDA of $95.7 million, or 14.7% of sales in Q4 2024.

Cash flow from operations was $62.9 million, representing 8.1% of net sales.

Total book in the quarter was 1.35. I had an A&D program backlog of $1.6 billion.

In Data Center Computing and Networking, the two accounted for total company revenues of 36%, driven by demand for generative AI.

Edwin Roks, President & CEO of TTM Technologies Inc., said this about the performance:

“We delivered another strong quarter with both revenues and non-GAAP EPS at or above the high end of the guided range. Revenues grew 19% reflecting continued demand strength in the Data Center Computing and Networking end markets supporting AI and data center buildouts. Our Medical, Industrial and Instrumentation end market experienced healthy double-digit revenue growth while Aerospace and Defense experienced higher than market growth.”

For full-year 2025, the company increased net sales to $2.9 billion, up $500 million from last year's full year net sales of $2.4 billion, up 19%.

GAAP operating income for full-year 2025 reached $264.7 million, compared to $116.00 million for full-year 2024.

GAAP net income for all of 2025 was $177.4 million, or $1.68 per diluted share, a big increase of GAAP income for all of 2024 of $56.3 million, or $0.54 per diluted share.

Adjusted EBITDA for full-year 2025 was $456.3 million, accounting for 15.7% of net sales. In full-year 2024 the company generated adjusted EBITDA of $351.5 million, or 14.4% of net sales.

The share price of the stock has a 52-week low of $15.77, and a 52-week high of $111.00. At the time of this writing the share price stood at $91.80.

What next for TTMI?

The company has had an extraordinary growth trajectory with its sales price, consistently moving up in value. But starting on January 9, 2026 it started to soar, climbing from about $65.00 per share to approximately $107.00 per share on January 15. It reached its 52-week high on February 3, and has since dropped roughly $20.00 per share to the $91.80 price mentioned above.

The question now is, is this a good entry point or should investors wait for a lower price to place a trade? My thesis is TTMI has a lot of growth left in it, and probably the best strategy would be to dollar-cost average in over time. Even if the share price drops I don't see long-term investors losing with this dynamic company.

AI infrastructure demand

Among the tailwinds supporting my thesis is first, AI infrastructure demand. Workloads in the market require advanced thermal management, complex interconnect systems, high‑density PCBs, and high‑frequency RF components. Demand for these should continue to grow for some time.

Within the AI infrastructure ecosystem, TTMI has positioned itself as a key hardware supplier. I don't think this has been fully valued into the company by the market in the ongoing AI infrastructure growth cycle.

Defense spending growthTTMI has been supplying RF components and mission systems to the U.S. defense programs. Those components target aerospace platforms, communications, electronic warfare, and radar systems.

When taking into account the current geopolitical environment, defense budgets continue increase, which will provide the company with lasting, high-margin revenue for the foreseeable future.

High‑performance computing & data centers

It's no secret that demand from cloud providers for data centers that are optimized by AI will remain in play for some time. That means demand for TTMI's PCBs and RF components will continue to increase.

The company's high-layer-count PCBs and other specialty materials should benefit strongly from the demand in the cloud market.

Operational efficiency & margin expansionTaking into account revenue growth is vastly outpaced by the earnings growth of the company, suggests several things:

The firm has terrific cost controls in place, is improving its product mix, and is gaining share in its defense and specialty components market, which generate very high margins. Margin expansion and operating leverage confirm this at the operational level.

Risk the company faces

I don't see the AI and defense portion of the company's business being under pressure over the next couple of years, but where the company could face some pressure would be in the cyclical automotive and industrial segments of its business.

With the growth trajectory the company has enjoyed, there could be a disproportionate response if its cyclical business slows down, which it eventually will.

Considering its PE (FWD) is 28.60, it means investors have high expectations for TTMI. If and when any type of slowdown in its major segments results in underperformance, it would result in an even bigger hit to the firm than would come from the cyclicals. That would mean some downward pressure on margins and EPS.One thing that could significantly disrupt the performance of the company would be in regard to any supply chain disruptions. Since the company has a complex supply chain, reflected in its reliance on global suppliers, specialty materials, and tight logistics, any prolonged and/or deep interruption could have a severe impact on the company.

On the competitive side of things, Asia could become a factor if and when pricing becomes important to customers. Since TTMI competes primarily in more complex products, I don't think that should be a factor at this time, but if Asia starts to compete in some of its higher-margin markets, it could drag the company down if it incrementally loses market share.

Conclusion

After soaring in valuation from early 2025 to now, the obvious question is whether or not the company represents good value as it stands today. Even though its PE is no longer cheap, I don't see its earnings coming under pressure for now. It should continue to benefit from AI infrastructure and defense demand. Its high-performance electronics products will also be a positive catalyst going forward.

The company has definitely had an extraordinary run over the last year, and while a lot of its current value is priced in, I have no doubt there's a lot more room to run, albeit at a more modest pace of growth.

With much of the deep value play having already occurred, the company is more of a moderate growth narrative at a decent price at this time.

Along with the obvious strength in defense and AI infrastructure, which produces strong margins and earnings, the company also has smaller but significant strengths in other segments, when combined together, should maintain its growth, although not at the pace it has been over the last year or so.

On the other hand, a higher valuation, recent accelerated growth, cyclical exposure in some of its markets, and some competition from Asian manufactures that compete on price, could result in some underperformance.When taken together, I still think TTMI has a lot of strength and growth inherent it, but expectations in comparison to its performance of the last year should be tempered.

data.title: This Is Not Your Father's Retirement: The 4% Rule Is Breaking
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

The 4% Rule Was Built for Owners, Not Renters

Traditional retirement strategies and plans were built for a world that no longer exists. The 4% rule and other similar strategies were made under the assumption that mortgages were paid off and major assets were owned outright. Expenses were predictable with occasional surprises, inflation was modest, and market volatility didn’t directly dictate monthly spending decisions.

Today’s reality is quite different. Households are increasingly indebted, and GenerationX is entering their 50s and 60s with the highest average debt of any generation. Don’t even get me started on millennials and GenZ, who are the prime customers for BNPL-type (Buy Now, Pay Later) financing arrangements to afford basics like phones, furniture, and burritos.

This isn’t a lifestyle critique. It’s a cash-flow reality.

We now live in a subscription economy where ownership has quietly been replaced by ongoing payments. What began with streaming services and cloud storage has expanded into phones, software, home security, printers, appliances, vehicles, and essential digital services. These are not indulgences; they are the price of participation in modern life. What begins as the price of a cup of coffee a day suddenly snowballs into a hundred coffees a day across healthcare, housing, technology, and services. Subscriptions are sticky, and inflation compounds subscriptions quietly but surely.

The modern retiree will carry several sticky monthly payments, from health, home, and auto insurance to utilities and digital services. Providers will raise prices each year, leaving retirees with little choice but to absorb the increase or forgo access altogether. When expenses are monthly and persistent, a portfolio built solely on future asset sales is structurally mismatched.

Two Investors, One Secret Advantage

Between 1955 and 1962, Marjorie Bradt received shares of AT&T Corporation valued at $6,626 as a gift from her father. She quietly signed up for the company’s dividend reinvestment plan, didn’t rebalance, didn’t trade, and didn’t follow the markets. In 1984, due to a federal antitrust lawsuit, “Ma Bell” was broken into multiple baby bells, each of which issued shares to Marjorie and paid her dividends in the form of additional shares. She reinvested everything, and by 1999, her inheritance had blossomed into a portfolio of ten stocks worth over $1 million. Source

Marjorie did not foresee the extent or the effects of the Vietnam War, the Gulf War, the inflationary crisis of the 70s, or other perils during her decades of being invested. She didn’t stress over the news or the narratives and didn’t predict anything. She quietly held and reinvested every dividend, letting the power of compounding do all of the work.

Almost in a similar timeline, a gas station attendant and janitor in a small Vermont town named Ronald Reid built a portfolio of dividend-paying companies for over 54 years. No one expected that at the time of his death in June 2014, his will would reveal an $8 million portfolio comprising at least 95 companies. Reid had diligently saved and invested for decades and left a generous gift of $6 million to his local library and hospital.

Reid and Bradt came from humble beginnings and didn’t have exotic holdings. They did not invest in cryptocurrency, or get lucky with blockbuster IPOs, or meme stocks. They didn’t trade options or time the market to perfection. They navigated economic challenges, saw their portfolio values rise and drop, but continued collecting steady dividend payments along the way, and diligently reinvested to harness the power of compounding.

Both these investors had very little in common socially, professionally, or financially, but they both built fortunes by owning assets that paid them regardless of what markets were doing. The key takeaway here is that they kept accumulating assets throughout their lives and never had to sell anything to support their cash flow needs.

Why I Love Being an Income Investor?

It is no surprise that at High Dividend Opportunities, we are passionate income investors. Whether it is a bull market or a bear market, we remain persistent buyers of income-producing securities. In ten years of writing on Seeking Alpha, I get asked this question many times: how many holdings do you have, and how do you keep buying everything you write about?

I own hundreds of securities in my personal portfolio, and the HDO model portfolio is a subset of that. Every holding in my portfolio produces a small passive stream of income, and as a large portfolio of hundreds of such holdings, I produce strong cash flows every month, every quarter, and every year, enabling me to be a buyer no matter what happens in the markets.

Investing in the financial markets carries risks, but diversification provides a strong shield. At HDO, we recommend holding at least 42 holdings, because studies have shown that holding somewhere between 30 and 50 securities almost makes business risk disappear.

We take this further by diversifying access to common equity, preferred equity, and baby bonds—through a combination of individual holdings and diversified funds.

Among the many reasons I love being an income investor, today I want to focus on five that matter most in a subscription-driven world.

1) Income Eliminates Market Timing Risk

Retirement does not fail because markets deliver poor long-term returns; it fails when cash is needed at the wrong time.

When expenses are recurring and unavoidable, relying on asset sales introduces timing risk into everyday living. High-income structures such as REITs, CEFs, and BDCs reduce that risk by delivering cash flow directly from portfolio holdings, independent of market price movements.

Let us take a look at one leader from each of the above asset classes—Ares Capital (ARCC), Realty Income (O), and Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG). If we examine their price changes in the past 20 years, it will seem like they are a waste of time, and the S&P 500 was an easy winner. After all, ARCC has barely moved; that makes it a terrible investment, right?

Wrong. Take a look at the total return (which includes dividends collected and reinvested along the way)—the tables are completely turned!

All of these securities have experienced market drawdowns and price fluctuations. The difference is how an investor interacts with those periods. A high-income portfolio provides cash flow without requiring asset sales. A portfolio dependent on price appreciation forces investors to sell shares when cash is required, and if that need arises during a market downturn, those shares are permanently removed from participation in any subsequent recovery.

Market risk, if simply put, is the fact that the markets may not cooperate when you need them to. Income does not eliminate market risk; it eliminates forced timing risk, which is often far more damaging in retirement.

2) Higher Income is Now a Structural Requirement

Even if the markets cooperate, our income needs are much higher today than in prior retirement cycles.

Previous generations entered retirement with a greater share of life’s essentials fully owned. Mortgages were paid off, vehicles were owned outright, and many recurring expenses naturally declined over time.

That dynamic has changed and continues to worsen. Today, fewer assets are fully owned, and more essentials are accessed through ongoing payments. The next generation will be entering retirement still owning debt (home, auto, education, healthcare, and several others) and will be tied to services that increasingly require continuous cash outflows. Moreover, advancements in healthcare have increased the overall life expectancy, meaning your capital needs to last longer.

While capital may still exist on paper, life’s expenses demand liquidity, not valuation. If you are planning on selling assets in your retirement, you will be running the risk of running out of assets when you need them the most.

The example we discussed in the beginning, with Marjorie Bradt and Ronald Reid—even in their advanced years, their equity ownership kept growing while producing massive income that they could tap into on demand. This forms the core element of our Income Method at HDO. We address the challenge by keeping core assets intact while generating sufficient, repeatable cash flow, allowing regular reinvestment so the same capital base produces progressively higher income over time.

Let us compare a new retiree’s $1 million investment in Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) with an equal-weighted investment in ARCC, O, and UTG (call it the Income Method), made in January 2008. Unfortunate timing, and you will be right to point out we may be cherry-picking the market top, but life happens, and there have been many unfortunate retirees who saw their portfolios collapse within months of embarking on this new chapter of their lives. Source

As you can see, between Jan 2008 and Dec 2015, that $1 million portfolio experienced a significant drawdown in both approaches. But looking at cash flows (assuming a 4% withdrawal rule), the Income Method consistently produced more income than withdrawals, positioning the retiree for continued reinvestment to participate in the market rebound. At the end of the eight years, the Income Method ended with a portfolio value of $1.7 million, while BRK.B stayed flat, leaving the investor with fewer shares than they started with.

When the withdrawal rate is increased to 6%, the contrast becomes more severe. The BRK.B portfolio enters a depletion trajectory, demonstrating how higher modern income requirements amplify the risks of asset-sale-dependent strategies, especially when unexpected expenses arise early in retirement. Source

If your retirement plan depends primarily on selling shares, caution is warranted. Markets can remain unfavorable longer than retirees can afford to liquidate their assets.

3) Inflation is Less Dangerous When Income Arrives First

During your working years, you likely competed with peers, other businesses, and entrepreneurs to get ahead in your career. In retirement, your primary nemesis is inflation. Your dollar buys less and less because things get more expensive slowly but surely.

If we model the same BRK.B vs. the HDO trio in the GFC (Global Financial Crisis) period, and assume the withdrawals are inflation-adjusted, then the selling-shares approach clearly demonstrates portfolio erosion over time. Source

Inflation is most damaging to retirees when it forces asset sales. Funding a rising cost of living by selling shares accelerates capital depletion, especially when inflation coincides with market volatility.

Higher starting yields also provide a natural buffer against rising expenses because your income consistently is in excess of the requirement, allowing you to reinvest the difference and snowball your income over time. Income investing does not eliminate inflation risk, but it helps prevent inflation from translating into a steadily shrinking lifestyle.

4) Volatility is a Lifestyle Risk, Not Just a Market Statistic

Volatility becomes a problem in retirement when it threatens spending stability. For income-focused strategies, market price fluctuations often have a limited impact on recurring cash flows, changing your overall experience in the markets during those uncomfortable times.

Let us examine this by looking at the same HDO trio vs. three successful and dominant technology companies that have delivered excellent long-term returns for shareholders—Amazon (AMZN), Zscaler (ZS), and ServiceNow (NOW). These were some of the biggest winners in the post-pandemic world and the biggest losers during the 2022 bear market. They returned to being big winners in the AI-fueled bull market, only to begin another collapse in mid-2025.

For a total-return-focused investor, the past five years have been dramatic but unproductive if no assets were sold at the right time. In contrast, income holdings such as UTG, ARCC, and O continued to generate cash flows throughout the volatility.

Unrealized gains can vanish quickly. Paid distributions cannot be taken back.

Total return equals price appreciation plus dividends. If most of that return comes from price alone, the outcome depends on selling assets at favorable prices, a risk retirees can’t always afford to take.

Income is tangible. It arrives regardless of market sentiment and supports daily life today, tomorrow, and forever, without requiring market timing. In retirement, the stability of cash flow matters more than the visual comfort of a growing account balance.

5) High Income Creates Flexibility—the Real Objective of Retirement

Well-diversified income strategies create optionality—the freedom to adapt to changing markets and life circumstances without destabilizing an entire retirement plan.

To gauge your readiness for retirement, ask yourself a few simple questions:

  • Can you step away from the markets for a few months to travel, pursue your passions, or simply disengage without needing to monitor daily prices?
  • Would your portfolio continue to function if illness, caregiving, or other life events temporarily prevented your active involvement?
  • What happens if your retirement begins earlier than planned due to job loss during an unfavorable market condition?
  • Can your portfolio remain solvent in a severe bear market, when you don’t have external capital to deploy?

Income-focused portfolios are designed to answer these questions with resilience. When cash flow continues to arrive, plans can bend without breaking, allowing retirees to navigate uncertainty without sacrificing long-term stability.

If your answer to these questions is no, you are not financially independent, and your retirement readiness may be more fragile than you think.

Conclusion

At High Dividend Opportunities, we don’t just build portfolios; we teach the discipline behind them. We help members understand how to construct rate-agnostic portfolios use bond ladders, preferred securities, baby bonds, and diversified funds to generate durable cash flow across market cycles. HDO recently completed 10 years on Seeking Alpha, and we remain humbled by the trust our members and readers place in us.

Regardless of market conditions, we are active buyers, continuously deploying capital to buy income-generating assets, because every dividend collected is a small victory, regardless of headlines and market sentiment. As the world pays endlessly for things it doesn’t own, income investors keep buying assets that keep paying them. This is the power of our Income Method, a winning strategy for a confident, flexible, and stress-free retirement.

data.title: Universal Health Services: Quality Operator, Hold At Current Levels
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

In my earlier piece on Universal Health Services (UHS), I focused on margin recovery, selective investments, and aggressive buybacks. At that time, upside was mostly driven by multiple expansion.

The stock has declined marginally since then till mid-January, but it then rebounded afterward and is currently trading at $235. Interestingly, returns are no longer tied to multiple expansion but rather execution, EPS growth, and further buybacks.

The Q3 performance supports the idea that the company can increase revenues, manage expenses, and maintain strong margins in both acute and behavioral segments.

Pricing power and operational effectiveness are keeping top-line momentum, while labor stabilization and disciplined spending are causing faster growth of profits compared to revenue.

Moreover, share buyback and a quarterly dividend of $0.20 signal the confidence and willingness of the management to return cash to the shareholders.

The full-year projections have been raised, indicating that Q4 and 2025 will end on an upbeat note, with stable volumes, declining labor expenses, and incremental Medicaid reimbursements. That said, execution will be essential because risks remain.

Furthermore, the stock is currently trading at fair value, which limits the future expansion of the multiple.

Based on all these aspects, I would still maintain a ‘hold’ rating on UHS stock.

Universal Health Services Q3 2025 Earnings

UHS posted robust Q3 earnings with solid momentum.

Its revenue increased by 13.4% annually to $4.5 billion, due to consistent patient demand and the capability to increase the pricing. It is nice to see that growth has not been pushed by volume alone, which makes results seem sustainable.

Even more impressive is its operating performance. Segment operating income increased from $338 million to $522 million in Q3, and nine-month totals have climbed to $1.48 billion. Margins increased as the revenue surpassed the expenses. The payoff from the previous labor stabilization activities is starting to be seen in the results.

Additionally, salaries, wages, and benefits rose with the revenue and other operating costs were efficiently increased with more patient volumes. Interest expense reduced by 13.9% to $38 million, helping pre-tax income increase by 45.5% to $497 million.

Meanwhile, net income surged 44.1% to $373 million, and diluted EPS rose 54.2% to $5.86. These results validate that operational improvements and share repurchases are tangibly benefiting the shareholders.

Alongside, operating cash flow clocked in at $1.29 billion for the nine months, a little less than the previous year. Capital expenditure was approximately $734 million, which means that the management is spending wisely. In the meantime, stock buybacks of $616 million strengthen business confidence.

In general, UHS is performing well and can withstand a complicated healthcare environment.

UHS Q4 and Full-Year Outlook

What's more crucial right now is to focus on the upcoming Q4 earnings and the full-year outlook.

UHS has an evident momentum and the management has increased full-year guidance on the basis of Q3 performance. Starting with revenues, the management has estimated full year revenue between$17.31-$17.4 billion, with adjusted EPS at $21.50-$22.10. It also referred to the Washington, D.C., Medicaid supplemental payment and approved a $1.5 billion increase to the stock buyback program, which indicates confidence in cash flow and business momentum.

Based on the management’s full-year outlook, I anticipate revenues in the range of $4.4 to $4.6 billion in Q4. I predicted the range by assuming same-facility growth and the normal seasonal trends.

In addition, adjusted EBITDA would clock in at approximately $650-$700 million, and adjusted EPS in the $5.4-$5.9 range. The margins must be stable because labor costs will be normalized, but reimbursement timing, and especially Medicaid and commercial payers can cause short-term fluctuations.

Besides this, UHS is continuing its track record of giving back capital to the shareholders in addition to buybacks. It announced a quarterly dividend of $0.20, payable March 16.

Overall, I expect UHS to end 2025 on a solid, disciplined note, justifying the momentum in Q3 while being exposed to the traditional healthcare execution and reimbursement risks.

UHS Stock Valuation and Price Point Analysis

UHS shares fell between the end of November 2025 and mid-January 2026 from around$244 to $199. This dip meant shifting investor sentiment, hesitant healthcare sector views, and slower post pandemic procedures volume trends. That said, the stock has since escalated to $235, which is notable given the sharp rally it provided in the second half of 2025.

Technically, the price action remains constructive. The stock is trading strongly above its key moving averages as seen in the snapshot above. Such alignment confirms a solid underlying trend and indicates that institutional support is still effective. When a stock is overextended against 200-day average, the upward trend tends to decelerate as the price becomes consolidated and the fundamentals catch up.

Therefore, downside appears to have been supported in the low-200s, but upside at the current point appears to be more positive than open-ended.

UHS is viewed as cheap on a surface level screen, but context is an important factor. It trades at around 10.7x forward earnings and 7.6x forward EV to EBITDA, which are significantly below industry averages. With that said, hospital operators structurally trade at discounted multiples due to reimbursement risk, labor volatility, and capital intensity.

This low P/CF ratio of 7.5x is partly explained by the noise in the working-capital and high-receivables other than long-term robust free cash flow. Meanwhile, the P/B ratio of approximately 2x suggests that the market is already knowledgeable about normalized profitability and balance sheet stability. In other words, UHS has become fairly valued rather than undervalued and there is limited further multiple expansion without a step-change in fundamentals.

Lastly, earnings expectations assist in setting realistic price outcomes over the coming 12 months. The estimated 2026 EPS of $23.39 indicates steady operations and buybacks.

Let’s begin by using a more conservative 10x multiple, which is suitable in the case of a mature hospital business. In this scenario, a base-case in the range of $235 to $240 is obtained, which is in line with the current price. This means that there is little upside in a steady-state situation.

Under a bull scenario, EPS might hit the upper end of estimates of about $24.70 assuming that the pricing power holds and labor costs are contained. Using a slightly higher 11x multiple on that figure justifies a potential upside target of between $265 and $270. Even then, the returns would be execution-based and not valuation expansion-based.

Risks

For starters, reimbursement timing and policy changes are one of the risks. Slowdowns or cutbacks in Medicaid or commercial payments can strain the cash flow. Although the company has overcome these problems in the past, sudden changes in government funding could potentially shrink the margins.

Then there are increasing labor costs. Although the contract labor costs are dropping, the operating leverage can be eroded as a result of wage inflation or labor shortages.

Furthermore, growth in revenues is now more price-driven than volume-driven. If the volumes of patients fail to rise or the payer negotiation loses strength, top-line momentum can be slackened. Acute and behavioral admissions are stable, yet demand or competition might impact profitability.

Last but not least, the stock is overextended and is trading at a fair value from market's perspective. Limited multiple expansion minimizes upside, meaning additional gains are dependent on operational performance as opposed to re-rating.

These pressures must be weighed against the disciplined operational performance of UHS.

Takeaway

So, what do we get?

UHS is a well-run company with consistent cash flow, healthy margins, and prudent capital management. Q3 performance and full-year outlook demonstrate that the business is capable of increasing revenues and controlling costs. Share repurchases and the quarterly dividend of $0.20 strengthens shareholder returns and offer reliable income.

That said, the share is currently fairly valued. The future gains will be based on further execution, not re-rating, and risks such as reimbursement timing, labor pressures, and seasonal swings remain. Right now, you should not chase UHS as a high-growth opportunity, but hold it for reliable income and steady-growth interest.

data.title: Janus Henderson Research Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio management

Joshua Cummings, CFA

John Jordan

Investment environment

  • U.S. shares rose on positive corporate earnings news. Economic growth has been relatively stable despite a government shutdown, policy headwinds, and slower-than-expected employment growth.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) followed a September rate cut with two additional 25 basis-point (bp) rate cuts, in October and December, respectively.
  • Excitement around artificial intelligence (AI) was a strong driver of returns early in the quarter. The market began to broaden in the second half of October as concerns over valuations and potential investment returns led to declines in AI-related stocks. Nonetheless, stocks ended the year on a strong note.

Portfolio review

Netflix (NFLX) was a relative detractor despite delivering double-digit revenue and earnings increases, higher guidance, and a growing subscriber base and engagement. Despite these results, the stock sold off on concerns over the relative valuation. Investors also worried that the company’s planned acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) could face regulatory hurdles or result in a bidding war. We continue to see long-term opportunity for Netflix given its scale, pricing power, and content library, which would be difficult for competitors to replicate. The company has continued to expand its customer base, especially internationally, and it has found more ways to monetize its platform through paid password sharing, price increases, and advertising. We continue to see a long runway for growth, supported by the company’s global, high-quality content and selective moves into live events and sports-adjacent programming.

Oracle (ORCL) was another relative detractor, amid volatility in AI-related stocks. Oracle has emerged as a leading player in the AI market, supported by its strong and growing position in the hyperscale market as well as close relationships with leading AI partners. Oracle’s cloud business has signed several multibillion-dollar contracts. This led to a large increase in remaining performance obligations (RPOs), which represent expected future revenues. Despite the company’s strong financial performance, the stock declined in the fourth quarter as investors grew more cautious about Oracle’s ability to fund the ambitious infrastructure buildout necessary to service these future contracts. Further, the market has concerns about the potential customer concentration within these RPOs. While we believe these concerns are real, we view the risk/reward as positive as an optionality sized position given the potential for Oracle to be a leader of the AI-capacity buildout, as well as its technological advantages and strategic business relationships.

Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) was a positive contribution in the information technology sector. The company manufactures specialized deposition and etching tools that are critical to producing the advanced semiconductors needed for AI. Lam has become a structural share gainer in wafer fabrication equipment (WFE) because of both its strong customer relationships and rising etch and deposition intensity across leading-edge foundry/logic, dynamic random-access memory (DRAM), and advanced packaging. Shares rose as investors grew more confident in Lam’s opportunity in the AI space, which has led to strong third-quarter results and expanded margins. The company also raised guidance as the management team indicated better-than-expected demand in the WFE market. We have continued to see long-term opportunity for Lam Research as a market leader in many of the technologies that enable AI.

Avidity Biosciences (RNA), another positive contributor, is developing next-generation therapies that target genetic drivers of rare neuromuscular diseases. We originally invested in Avidity because of our confidence in its management team, differentiated pipeline, and proprietary technology that generated compelling data in diseases like myotonic dystrophy that showed disease modification. Our conviction paid off when global pharmaceuticals company Novartis announced its acquisition of Avidity, leading to a jump in the stock price.

Manager outlook

Equity markets have continued to rally on strong corporate earnings and resilient U.S. economic growth. Investors have moved away from their worst-case fears about U.S. trade policy, which has boosted the performance of risk assets. We also have been encouraged by stable economic growth outside of the U.S., especially in Europe, which may offer opportunities for companies with international exposure. Many global central banks are now in easing mode, which has been another tailwind for equity market performance. While the extent of easing will depend on the interplay of economic growth and inflation, lower interest rates could reduce funding costs and provide renewed support for sectors such as residential construction.

While we continue to monitor economic developments and their impact on our companies, our approach aims to minimize broader macro and style factors while we focus instead on bottom-up stock selection. The foundation of our fundamentals-driven investment process is our exceptional team of analysts, who bring deep knowledge of their respective sectors. Our analyst team is constantly on the lookout for companies with strong or improving business models, exceptional management teams, and underappreciated competitive advantages. Through our time-tested, disciplined investment approach, we will continue to pursue our goal of long-term growth in capital.
